headline: Goalkeeper's World Cup Dream Lives On in Sea Slug Species
meta_description: A biologist names a newly discovered red sea slug after Cape Verde's goalkeeper Vozinha to honour his World Cup heroics against Spain.
summary_short: Cape Verde's goalkeeper Vozinha gains an unexpected honour: a newly discovered sea slug species named after him by a passionate marine biologist.
body:
When Cape Verde's goalkeeper Vozinha kept a goalless draw against World Cup holders Spain, he did more than secure his nation's first World Cup point. He inspired a marine biologist to immortalise him in the ocean.

Jesus Ortea, a professor emeritus at the University of Oviedo, discovered a small red mollusc in the Caribbean and chose to name the new species Aldisa vozinha. The decision honoured both Vozinha's "prominent role" in Cape Verde's stunning World Cup campaign and a fitting detail: the sea slug's striking red colour echoes the nickname of Spain's team, La Roja—The Red One.

"The red colour of the dedicated species is a reminder of his feat," Ortea explained in his discovery report.

Vozinha's performance that night transformed his life overnight. His Instagram followers surged from 50,000 to 17.4 million—surpassing even NFL legend Tom Brady.

Cape Verde, the second smallest nation ever to reach the World Cup finals, eventually exited in the last 32 after an extra-time defeat to Argentina. Yet Vozinha's legacy extends far beyond the pitch. At 75, Ortea has previously named discoveries after other football heroes, including Costa Rica's goalkeeper Keylor Navas. For the 40-year-old goalkeeper, a sea slug bearing his name stands as proof that heroism resonates across worlds.
